Spring 2026 Project

Author

Theresa Louise Binnings

Data Description

This data comes from the National Science Foundation, specifically the National Center for Science and Engineering Statistics. The data tables used were nsf22310-tab001-005 (showing data from 2019) and nsf25322-tab001-005 (showing data from 2023) - both titled “Employed scientists and engineers, by sex, major field of highest degree, ethnicity, race, disability status, and type of disability.”

The tables exhibit survey data from 2019 and 2023, capturing the count of individuals in various generalized STEM fields. The table then looked at the count of individuals categorized by sex (male/female), and then ethnicity/race within these 2 sex categories, and the generalized rows (where no sex was defined). Additionally the table looks at the count of individuals with disabilities working in these generalized STEM fields.

Data Cleaning

Transformation of this data began by reading in the excel document and skipping the first 15 rows. This allowed for manual labeling of the columns by skipping the subheading format of the excel document and it cut off the part of the table that looked at generalized counts for race/ethnicity and disabilities without any sex (male/female) delineation. The table was further cropped to exclude the disability columns since the focus of these questions and visualizations centered on sex and race/ethnicity representation in STEM. The non-zero count values delineated with “*“,”D”, or “S” were transformed into missing NA values as the data was read in.

The original excel data spread the different race/ethnicity labels across the table into columns. In order to clean the data, these values were pivoted so that each count could be seen as a distinct row designated by a single “count” column, labelled with the appropriate “race/ethnicity” column associated with each count.

A “sex” column was added that designated rows associated with each sex; the original data table used a subheading on the first column to indicate the two sections with male/female data. Extraneous “total count” values that represented summed values of all race/ethnicities for each field were filtered out.

The column for race/ethnicity was compiled to group the smaller counts into a single “Other” category so that these counts could be seen in the final graphic relative to the larger values associated with “Black or African American”, “Asian”, “Hispanic or Latino”, and “White”. The new “Other” category included count values associated with “American Indian or Alaska Native”, “Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander”, and “Multiracial” labels for race/ethnicity.

Finally, a proportion column was added and calculated to show the proportion of each race/ethnicity within the two sex categories relative to the total of all counts to showcase how the quantity of men and women of various race/ethnicities are represented in STEM as a whole.

Additionally a column designating the year for each table was added. The two tables were joined in order to create the graphic showcasing each bar graph next to one another.

Data Visualization 1

These two circular bar charts compare the distribution of employed scientists and engineers in 2023 by sex, field of study, and race/ethnicity. The contrast between the larger male chart on the left and the smaller female chart on the right immediately reflects the broader imbalance between men and women in STEM fields. Engineering and Computer and Mathematical Sciences show the most dramatic difference, with both fields having a greater number of employed individuals overall and those individuals being predominantly men.

These circular charts also highlight that within the underrepresentation of women in science, there is a broader underrepresentation of diverse races and ethnicities across the STEM workforce. Across both charts, White employees make up the majority, with Asian employees representing the next largest group. However, Hispanic or Latino, Black or African American, and other racial and ethnic groups remain significantly underrepresented in both the male and female charts across all fields.

Data Visualization 2

These two barplots show how the proportion of men and women in major STEM fields and the representation within the broad race/ethnicity categories has changed from 2019 to 2023. While slight shifts can be seen between the two barcharts, the graph shows the continued dominance of male representation as well as white racial dominance in these fields.

When one begins to look at the history of science deeper and recognize the advancements that have come as women entered the arena of science, it becomes clear that science advances dramatically with the incorporation of diverse backgrounds and perspectives. These charts showcase how we must still work to create diverse scientific arenas to continue to foster greater scientific exploration, advancement and equity for all beings and spaces.
